I'm a 45-year-old man in okay health. A few times this week after a big meal I've had this weird pressure in my chest. I'm pretty sure it's just bad heartburn, but is there a way to be sure it's not something more serious?

It's not possible to be sure on your own, so you should get evaluated by a doctor to determine the cause of your chest pressure. While it might be heartburn, the symptoms of a heart attack can feel very similar, including pressure, fullness, or a squeezing sensation. If your chest pressure is accompanied by shortness of breath, sweating, or pain that spreads to your arm, neck, or jaw, seek emergency medical care immediately.

My two-year-old has a 103°F fever from a cold. I've heard that fever is the body's way of fighting infection and that using a fever reducer can get in the way of that. Should I just let it run its course?

It is recommended to give your child a fever reducer like acetaminophen (Tylenol) or ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin) if they seem uncomfortable, fussy, or are not drinking well. The main goal of treating a fever is to improve your child's comfort, and there is no strong evidence that bringing a fever down hinders their ability to fight off a common cold. In addition to medication, you can help by encouraging your child to drink plenty of fluids and get rest.

I feel awful—sore throat, sinus pressure, the works. It feels exactly like the sinus infection I had last year. I still have some of the amoxicillin the doctor gave me then. Is it okay to just take that instead of going back to the doctor?

No, you should not take leftover amoxicillin. It is very important to see a healthcare provider for an accurate diagnosis, as most sinus infections are caused by viruses, which antibiotics do not treat. Taking antibiotics when they are not needed can cause unnecessary side effects and contributes to the serious public health problem of antibiotic resistance. A provider can determine the cause of your symptoms and prescribe the correct treatment.

I've been on an SSRI for my depression for about six months and I'm feeling completely back to my old self. Honestly, I feel great. Can I just stop taking the pills now? I don't want to be on medication if I don't need it.

It's wonderful that you're feeling so much better, but you should not stop taking your SSRI on your own. Suddenly stopping an antidepressant can cause uncomfortable withdrawal-like symptoms and increases the risk of your depression returning. Please talk with the doctor who prescribed your medication; if you both agree it's the right time, they will help you create a plan to taper off the dose safely and gradually.

For the last couple of days, I've been getting this dull, aching pain in the center of my chest. It seems to come and go, especially after I eat. I'm a 48-year-old man and a bit overweight. I'm pretty sure it's just bad heartburn, right?

You should have this chest pain evaluated by a doctor right away. While your symptoms could be from heartburn, a dull, aching pain in the center of the chest can also be a sign of a serious heart condition, and it's impossible to tell the difference without a medical exam. Given your age and risk factors, it is not safe to assume this is something minor.
My two-year-old has a fever of 104°F. She's fussy and sleepy but otherwise seems okay. I'm getting really scared by the number on the thermometer. What should I do?

A fever of 104°F is a reason to call your child's doctor for advice right away or to seek immediate medical care. While a high number on the thermometer is understandably frightening, the key is to have a professional determine the cause, especially since your daughter is also very fussy and sleepy. The doctor can provide a diagnosis and recommend the best course of action to help her feel more comfortable and start to get better.
I think my child might have an ear infection. He's been pulling on his ear and is really fussy and has a slight fever. Do I need to rush to the doctor for antibiotics?

It's best to have your child checked by a doctor to confirm an ear infection and get the right treatment plan. While the symptoms you describe are common for an ear infection, not all cases require antibiotics, as many can clear up on their own. A doctor can determine the best approach, which may be watchful waiting or an antibiotic if the infection appears to be bacterial and severe. In the meantime, you can help manage your child's discomfort with an over-the-counter pain reliever like acetaminophen or ibuprofen (for children over 6 months), following the dosing instructions for your child's age and weight.
